data.title: Arbor Realty's Huge 16% Yield: Don't Fold Yet
data.description:
Article Thesis

Arbor Realty Trust (ABR) is a mortgage REIT that is currently offering a very high dividend yield of 16%, following a steep sell-off in recent months. While the dividend is at risk of being cut -- which isn't surprising, considering its very high yield -- Arbor Realty Trust also has advantages, as it trades at a steep discount to book value, and since it could benefit from interest rate reductions in 2026. Overall, I believe that Arbor Realty Trust is not a low-risk investment at all, but investors could see compelling returns if things go right.

Past Coverage

I have written about Arbor Realty Trust here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in April 2025, when I published this article. Since then, Arbor Realty Trust has lowered its dividend from $0.43 to $0.30 per quarter, which has not well-received by income investors, but the stock also got cheaper on a price-to-book basis. With ABR's dividend yield now rising to 16% again, despite the dividend cut last year, it is time for me to take another look at the company today.

Arbor Realty Trust: Macro Environment

Arbor Realty Trust is a mortgage REIT, which naturally means that the company is heavily influenced by interest rate movements. Interest rate movements impact Arbor Realty Trust in several ways: First, interest rate movements impact demand for mortgages -- when interest rates decline, more people will buy a home, all else equal, meaning more money will be borrowed/more mortgages will be issued. Second, interest rate movements influence what the yield on new mortgages will be -- declining interest rates mean lower rates on newly issued mortgages, all else equal. Third, interest rate movements have an impact on the rates Arbor Realty Trust itself has to pay on the money it borrows when it issues new debt to refinance expiring debt or to grow its portfolio. And last but not least, interest rate movements impact the value of the company's investments -- when interest rates decline, the value of fixed-rate investments, such as mortgages, bonds, etc., increases, while the value of such investments declines when interest rates rise, all else equal.

Looking at 2025, we see that the Federal Reserve lowered interest rates several times, with the last cut happening in 2025. For the current year, further interest rate reductions are expected to fall further, with forecasts seeing 3 more 25 base point cuts this year. While there is, of course, no guarantee that this will happen, I think that it makes for a reasonable base case -- so what would this mean for Arbor Realty Trust? If interest rates continue to decline this year, the value of ABR's loan portfolio should climb to some degree, although this could be partially offset by bad debt write-offs. Arbor Realty Trust also could see advantages when it refinances debt it has issued in the past, although the yield on newly invested money will decline as well. In total, I think that the positives for Arbor Realty outweigh the negatives if interest rates fall this year, as the positive book value effect that declining interest rates will have seems most important to me. That being said, if there are fewer interest cuts compared to what is expected right now, e.g. due to higher-than-expected inflation, investors may be disappointed -- this could cause additional selling pressure.

Arbor Realty Trust: Recent Results

The company reported its most recent earnings results late last year, showing much better profits compared to what was expected, with distributable earnings coming in at $0.35 per share or $1.40 annualized -- which makes for an earnings yield of ~18%. This strong profit performance was caused by a major gain of almost $50 million on an equity investment -- an item that doesn't appear regularly, so this was a bit of a surprise. The one-time nature of a considerable part of ABR's profits during the period also means that forward profits will likely be lower, as it is unlikely that the company will report similar gains every quarter going forward. Adjusted for this one-time gain, profitability would have been way lower -- the company wouldn't have covered its dividend if not for this equity gain.

Looking at issuance activity, things looked pretty solid -- Arbor Realty Trust originated agency loans worth $1.98 billion during the most recent quarter, which was ABR's strongest quarter since Q4 2020. The company's servicing portfolio grew as well, rising to a little more than $35 billion, up 4% on a sequential basis. I think this suggests that ABR is doing fine on the issuance and business generation side, although things looked worse when it comes to credit quality. The company had 25 non-performing loans at the end of the most recent quarter, up from 19 non-performing loans one quarter earlier. The value of the non-performing loans, in total, also rose by more than $90 million. When it comes to asset quality, the trend thus is negative, making future write-downs and credit losses more likely, I believe -- investors definitely should keep an eye on these metrics in the future.

Arbor Realty Trust: Dividend And Valuation

Right now, Arbor Realty's dividend yield stands at 16%, based on a per-share dividend of $1.20 per year and a share price of around $7.70. This is, of course, very enticing, but I think that there is a meaningful risk of a dividend cut in the future. Arbor Realty cut its dividend in 2025, and during the most recent quarter, Arbor Realty would not have covered its dividend if not for the equity investment gain the company recorded. While a dividend cut is, of course, not set in stone, it seems possible for sure, so investors shouldn't see ABR as a safe dividend investment at all. That being said, even if Arbor Realty cuts its dividend, the yield would likely remain high -- another 30% dividend cut, like the one from 2025, would make its dividend yield decline to around 11%, which would still be pretty high. Even a pretty huge 50% dividend cut would mean that Arbor Realty would still yield around 8% -- far from bad. While a dividend cut is very much possible, I thus do not think that a dividend cut that is so big that it would make ABR's dividend yield drop to 2%, 3%, or even 5% is likely.

Arbor Realty's hefty dividend payments in the recent past have made its per-share book value decline slightly, from $12.99 at the end of March 2023 to $12.08 as of the end of the most recent quarter -- a decline of 7% over ten quarters, or a little less than 1% per quarter. Its share price has fallen way more, though, which is why ABR's valuation is now pretty low:

Shares trade for around 0.64x book value today, which is, apart from a very short period during the COVID crash, the lowest valuation over the last five years. I do not think that a book value multiple of more than 1.0x will come back any time soon, due to recent book value declines and due to uncertainties around the dividend, but it is definitely possible that ABR's book value multiple expands over the coming years. While book value could continue to decline slightly, investors may thus still see some share price gains -- e.g. if its book value multiple were to expand to a still not demanding 0.8x over the next couple of years.

Final Thoughts

Arbor Realty Trust has recently been a bad investment -- shares fell hard over the last couple of months, driven by fears about a dividend cut. And a dividend cut in 2026 is possible for sure -- but I think that the yield would likely remain high even after a dividend reduction.

Arbor Realty's valuation has fallen quite a lot, making shares pretty inexpensive today. Insiders continue to like the stock, with them loading up on shares in recent years -- it looks like they see value here:

I think Arbor Realty Trust is not a low-risk pick at all, but between the very high starting yield, the low valuation, insiders seeing value in ABR, and potential tailwinds from declining rates this year, Arbor Realty Trust still could be appealing for enterprising investors that don't mind the risks of an ABR investment.

data.title: TSMC: Powers America's AI Boom - Yet, The Valuation Doesn't Make Sense
data.description:
TSMC: The Most Underrated AI Play

If there was one semiconductor stock that I've truly regretted not adding a lot more into my personal portfolio, it has got to be Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) stock. Affectionately known among industry insiders as TSMC, the brainchild of founder Morris Chang, the foundry's leading status among its semi peers is very much enshrined, and all the more so, as the AI boom catapulted its ascendency into the pantheon of the AI greats of our generation.

My decision to upgrade TSM stock back to a Buy in my previous update was the right thing to do, but still somewhat late. In retrospect, I shouldn't have downgraded TSMC stock to a Neutral/Hold position, as I didn't quite fully appreciate just how important TSMC's technological stack and capabilities are to America's AI ambitions to help maintain the daylight between it and its Chinese peers. Because when AI becomes a geopolitical tool, this AI race will no longer just be solely dominated by politics, but also potentially leveraged to reduce the chances of America's adversaries even having the opportunity to leapfrog the U.S. in the realm of AI.

Why does this matter? Well, of course is matters. If it didn't matter, would Taiwan be so amenable to securing a deeper foothold while having TSMC invest more CapEx into its Arizona fabs, accounting for most of the total $250B in investments? That's a gargantuan show of strength from the world's foremost AI foundry that it's determined to make America feel comfortable amidst the battle royale between the world's two biggest economies, as they wrestle not just to own the AI narrative's sake, but also to determine the future trajectory of their relationship in this incredibly crucial sphere.

TSM Stock Looks Ready To Regain Leadership

Hence, I'm not at all surprised that TSM has been consolidating against the semi peers (SMH) constructively (as seen in the above chart). It has validated my conviction that owning a larger piece of TSM to partake in the AI boom isn't just an optionality (good to have), but a truly essential piece if we fully understand the puts and takes when contemplating what's at stake here, not only for America's AI companies.

At Taiwan Semi's fourth quarter earnings call earlier this month, there were worries about whether Intel (INTC) could be sizing up opportunities to lead with its 14A process. Management has also been keen to level up on that optimism, which I also enunciated in my most recent INTC article, as I dissected why the feverish sentiments on the stock have gone way overboard.

For now, the company is still banking on its potential to begin risk production from late 2027, while prognosticating volume production from some time in 2028. That should help keep interested investors on board, as we all know that the market runs (mostly) on clarity, and lower ambiguities are pivotal to help INTC justify its premium multiples right now. However, does that also place Intel's recovery at substantive risks if these timelines cannot be executed accordingly?

A closer introspection of TSMC's earnings call would have afforded some insights into the pathways that lay ahead not only for the Taiwan-based foundry, but also for Intel. TSMC Chair and CEO C.C. Wei provides the clues:

Let me explain a little bit because in these days, it's not a money to help you to compete, right? I also like whoever you just mentioned, to invest on Intel, I like them to invest on TSMC also. But the most fundamental thing is let me share with you. Today's technology is so complicated. So once you want to design a very complete or advanced technology, it takes 2 to 3 years to fully utilize that technology. That's today's situation. And so after 2 to 3 years of preparation, you can design your product. Once you get your product being approved, it takes another 1 to 2 years to ramp it up. So we have a competitor, no doubt about it. That's a formidable competitor. But first, it takes time; two, we don't underestimate their progress. But are we afraid of it? For 30-some years, we're always in a competition with our competitors. So no, we have confidence to keep our business grow as we estimate. - TSMC Q42025 earnings call

TSMC's CapEx Outlay Is A Competitive Edge

Of course, we don't have to take the words of one senior executive as the holy grail, and we shouldn't. However, facts are facts, and these timeline aspects are well-established in the industry. Building a fab isn't akin to going to Walmart (WMT) by grabbing a basket and then loading up our groceries. The eye-popping amount of money (Wall Street forecasts >$150B by TSMC between FY2025-27) that goes into such construction, and not forgetting the subsequent time and resources to be expended on testing, validation, and then into HVM or high volume production, goes through a laborious, arduous, but mostly carefully calibrated process.

Therefore, if Intel were serious about their 14A tech stack, they must provide the names now (customer order list), to placate the worries that it will not be another "overpromising and underdelivering" once again. At the current multiples (>85x forward earnings) that INTC is trading at, it does seem to me that the market has gone way out of character in validating Intel's "success" well ahead of time, when so much has yet to be proven.

Market Is Still Unduly Concerned With TSMC's China Risk

In contrast, TSM is just trading at slightly above 23x on forward earnings. Am I reading it wrong? Is the world really so fascinated and unduly worried about the high CapEx risks that the foundry must undertake, while also shifting more fabs to the U.S. in the next few years to make Arizona a production hub, and help America revive its bid to become a world class advanced semiconductor manufacturing base again? I used to think that TSMC could suffer not just in terms of costs but also yields, two key factors that could really torpedo TSMC's efforts to maintain its heavyweight champion status in AI foundry.

Yet, TSMC reminds us that it is so determined to sort out these nitty-gritty details that yields in the Arizona-based fabs are now competitive with those based in Taiwan. And well, that's a truly remarkable achievement, because as far as I'm concerned, Intel's still struggling to level up their yields for their 18A process, which also had an impact on their margins. C.C. Wei makes clear to us:

We built a fab in Arizona, and we work hard. So today, everything, even the yield or defect density is almost equal to Taiwan. And due to the strong demand, as I just answered from the AI stronger, that's a megatrend. All my customer and AI customers in the U.S., so they ask a lot of support from the U.S. fab. So because of that, we have to speed up our fab expansion in Arizona. In Taiwan also actually, we increased most of the capacity in Taiwan. No doubt about it because this is the most adjacent one we can progress very well. In the U.S., we try to speed it up and the progress is very good. We got the help from the government. But still, we have to meet all the requirement for the permits, for those kind of things. And so both in Taiwan and in Arizona, we speeded up our capacity expansion to meet the AI demand. - TSMC Q42025 earnings call.

Therefore, I'm under no illusion that even the U.S. government recognizes the importance of having TSMC producing at scale on its shores. In other words, it's not likely that the U.S. will pressure TSMC to cede production to Intel, and I don't even think that's what the hyperscalers and big tech will contemplate. There's little time to waste, and no resources to spurn if the U.S. wants to scale their compute capabilities quickly in their bid to stay ahead of their Chinese counterparts, who are innovating very quickly, despite facing an inherent compute disadvantage (due to U.S. chips export restrictions).

Therefore, I don't quite concur with the implied discount being applied on what is arguably the most critical player in the AI value chain (what's the point if we can't fab those chips at scale?) for Nvidia (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Meta Platforms (META), Google (GOOGL, GOOG), Broadcom (AVGO), and most recently, Microsoft (MSFT) as it unveils the Maia 200 AI chip, in an effort to retake the narrative on custom AI chips. I'm skeptical that these companies are voting for change (of foundry partner), and despite the "strategic price increases" by TSMC to reflect the value proposition and silicon content costs, the potential to dominate the AI race and not end up losing the "war" is monumental, and too consequential for any CEO to lose on their watch. For TSM, having the ability to commit such gargantuan amounts into CapEx is the moat that everyone else is missing, and the market will be wise not to underplay the opportunity for a valuation re-rating to even greater heights.

Nevertheless, this isn't tantamount to saying that the cross-straits tensions between China and Taiwan will automatically disappear. Piling on to these worries is the current state of play between China and Japan, which is unequivocally linked to the so-called "Taiwan contingency."

And, the unusual upheavals seen in China's Central Military Commission, as the purge of China's top general Zhang Youxia, despite having longstanding ties and close links to Chinese President Xi Jinping, have also stunned political and China watchers. The effects on China's Taiwan calculus remain uncertain, and therefore, I believe the market will not abruptly lower the implied threats to TSMC's fabs located in Taiwan, requiring an appropriate discount to be baked into the equation.

Therefore, the "balance of power" between the bulls and the bears will undoubtedly be predicated on whether the AI boom has a longer runway than what those bubble pessimists have forecasted. And if that's the case, then I believe TSM stock should be on its way to overtaking a new high not too long later.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: GLD: Never Follow Narratives Or Chase Momentum
data.description:
Gold is finally having its day in the sun, and human nature remains unchanged even in the face of all the information we have available today. The issue that I am referring to is how most people still think about the precious metal and the potential problem that this could create for their future portfolio returns.

For the past 5-year period, the most popular gold ETF - the SPDR® Gold Shares ETF (GLD)-has skyrocketed by almost 168%, while the S&P 500 delivered less than half of that. Gold's performance also seems to be experiencing something of a melt-up phase, with most market participants now catching up with the reality that has been with us for many years, and as usual, this is causing some hype around the yellow metal.

By simply observing this performance in the light of nosebleeding equity valuations, one should feel unease as opposed to ecstatic. Gold as an asset class is your bread-and-butter risk-off asset, while equities are usually near the other end of the spectrum. Especially high beta technology stocks, which have gone up in the stratosphere as of late.

People often think about all-asset bubbles and currency debasement when trying to make sense of this phenomenon, and that is true, but we should not forget that, as opposed to equity valuations, which are linked to actual business fundamentals, gold does not have a reference value. That is why investors should abstain from putting an exact number on where either gold or the GLD should trade.

Another consequence is that the sentiment plays a much bigger role for the price of gold, and as it happens, retail investors might be losing their minds over the sudden need to buy gold.

As someone who has been bullish on gold for many years and someone who still holds gold as a counterweight to his equity portfolio, this trend worries me even as long-term fundamentals for the precious metal remain supportive.

That is why for anyone who is now thinking about adding gold to their exposure, it is essential to have the right investment approach to begin with.

Don't Chase Short-Term Performance

We hear about the merits of being a long-term investor all the time when it comes to equities, but this is even more important for gold because it is subject to much longer cycles than equities are.

More specifically, over the past 100 years or so, gold experienced roughly 4 major bull markets. They differ in duration - from about a year to more than a decade. The underlying reason for each one of them, however, has been the same - the perceived risk for the existing monetary regime. I won't go into all the details for each of these periods, but for anyone interested in reading more on the topic, I did a deeper dive on the topic a few years ago.

The perceived risk for the monetary regime could take different forms - from high inflation and negative interest rates, which are definitive signs that the system is not working properly, to a simple change of the existing global regime that is necessary to happen from time to time.

Over the past decade, we have fallen within the first category with record highs in negative-yielding debt, highly unconventional monetary policies, and record inflation. Those were all signs that the monetary regime was experiencing some problems, and as a result, the GLD was occasionally outperforming the equity market.

The recent melt-up in the price of gold, however, is a clear sign that we have likely moved away from the initial symptoms, and market participants are now preparing for a more dramatic shift.

The graph with Google searches we saw above also confirms that retail investors are now fully on board with owning gold, which is most likely a direct result of some fear of missing out on gold's spectacular performance as of late.

What this shows is that more people are trying to time the market by betting on a large systemic event or are simply chasing short-term performance based on momentum. While the latter strategy could work in equity markets, for example, where share price momentum is often backed by business momentum, which tends to persist in the near to medium term, this is not the case for gold. The price of gold is not linked to any fundamentals, and this could easily result in significant volatility during times when market participants move either in or out of their gold positions.

With that in mind, it is very important to properly understand the long-term drivers for gold and set your expectations right.

Having the Right Approach

First and foremost, it is important for gold investors to disassociate themselves from narratives.

In the media, each gold price movement needs to be linked to some recent event in order to explain the movement. Such one-off events, however, are often irrelevant for the price of the precious metal and are simply used as excuses to create an appealing narrative.

Additionally, the gold price movements are also linked to some other variables, such as the strength of the U.S. dollar or the level of real interest rates. These work in explaining certain daily changes in the price of gold when nothing else is happening, but they are also irrelevant when it comes to explaining long-term trends.

The link between gold and real interest rates, for example, does exist during periods when perceived risk for the monetary regime is very low, but the relationship cannot be relied upon to formulate a proper investment strategy. I analyzed this phenomenon in further detail in an article all the way back in 2022, but the conclusion is still relevant.

Back in early 2022, the fear of rising real interest rates and the impact that this was expected to have on gold were widespread. Most market commentators had their investment theses revolve around the notion that higher rates would result in the price of gold falling sharply.

Fast forward to today, and indeed real interest rates are now much higher, but so is the price of gold.

Changes in exchange rates are also poor predictors for the price of gold eventually. There are times when these could be utilized in investors' favor, like the period in late 2022 (see the graph below) when the stronger U.S. dollar was indeed a headwind for the GLD.

Overall, however, exchange rates are a poor predictor of the price of gold and not a reason to either go long or sell the GLD.

The right approach towards gold is to use it as an addition to hold it over long periods of time as an addition to a diversified portfolio to hedge for risk related to the existing monetary system that could negatively affect risk assets. Avoid using narratives or specific price targets as either a reason to buy or hold and never rely on price momentum to be sustained.

Investor Takeaway

As opposed to other bull markets in gold, this time around retail investors seem to be particularly excited about the prospects of future returns. This comes at a time when the price of the precious metal is experiencing an unprecedented increase, and this short-term optimism could quickly disappear, even though the long-term tailwinds for gold remain. The reason is that the recent increase in price is comparable to only a handful of times during the past 100 years, and in each of these periods, we had a very important systemic event. This time around, such an event has not occurred yet, even though the symptoms have been with us for more than a decade.

data.title: Lumentum's AI Optics Business Got A Major Boost
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Scale-up and scale-out networks are in the initial innings of the next phase in AI networking, where DC (data center) architects solve for not only lower DC build costs, a.k.a. TCO (total cost of ownership), but also lessen the complexities in DC networks, which could create performance bottlenecks soon if not checked now.

This growing shift in AI networking’s architectural requirements is buttressing a tiny revolution from copper-based networking solutions to optical solutions, putting companies like Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE) at the center of the optical revolution that is beginning to play out in the data center.

And that optical revolution appears to be gathering pace based on news about another hyperscaler looking to integrate optical components in their data centers.

This news adds another layer of credibility to Lumentum, which already counts at least one hyperscaler in its client roster, giving its optical business a major boost, as I explain below.

I am initiating my coverage on Lumentum Holdings with a bullish view.

Why Does Lumentum’s Optical Solutions Matter in Data Centers Now

Optical solutions were always around, with Alphabet’s Google (GOOGL) being the first cloud company to use 10G optical transceivers in their data centers, and Lumentum became Google’s first supplier of these transceivers, per Lightcounting.

But over time, and through a strategic acquisition, Lumentum widened its portfolio of optical networking solutions and photonics offerings to include higher-margin networking components like lasers, putting them on a firm path to deliver their fastest ever period of top-line growth. As per consensus estimates, analysts are projecting a 61% jump in Lumentum’s FY26 revenues to $2.64B, their fastest ever in the public history of the company’s books. This can all be attributed to surging revenues from their Cloud & Networking revenue segment, which accounted for 86% of the company’s revenue base in FY25, up from ~75% in FY23, per the last known annual filings.

Such is the demand for Lumentum’s optical modules and laser components & subsystems from their Cloud & Networking customers that Lumentum’s management collapsed both these revenue segments into one consolidated segment to consolidate all the company’s resources on their higher-margin customers, like hyperscalers.

Certain market estimates do project Google to account for 21% of Lumentum’s revenue base. Combine this along with the news that I mentioned in the Thesis section above about another hyperscaler, i.e., Meta Platforms (META), inking a $6B deal with Corning (GLW), and we have strong credibility for another hyperscaler to enter the optical adoption phase in AI DC networking. That’s because Meta’s $6B deal signals a loud shift towards adopting Corning’s fiber optic cables that are complementary to the lasers, optical modules, and subsystems that Lumentum manufactures for their Cloud & Networking customers.

In addition to hyperscalers increasingly warming up to the increased likelihood of integrating optical components and solutions in data centers, Lumentum’s biggest vote of approval for its optical components comes from Nvidia (NVDA). Nvidia has already announced Lumentum as one of the primary vendors for its laser components that will be integrated in Nvidia’s Spectrum-X photonics-based switches. Nvidia further announced that their Spectrum-X switches will begin shipping in H2 of this year.

Investors should note that Lumentum is the dominant market leader for manufacturing EMLs (externally modulated lasers) that are routinely deployed in data center networks. At a conference last month, Lumentum’s management revealed they command “somewhere between 50% and 60% of the world's EML volume.” Additionally, Lumentum is also working on supplying ultra-high-powered lasers as well, which will eventually be used alongside copper-based solutions in scale-up and scale-out networks. Lumentum also provides CW (continuous wave) lasers, which some analysts believe are also being used by Nvidia in their CPO ambitions for switches and GPUs.

This leaves Lumentum with a unique combination of supplying a broad range of optical components, lasers, subsystems, and modules to semiconductor chipmakers like Nvidia as well as supplying components directly to the customers of these chipmakers, i.e., hyperscaler customers.

I believe both these customer cohorts are coming together perfectly for Lumentum at a time when demand is still on track to skyrocket further. This is also reflected in Lumentum’s quarterly accounts receivable, which surged 22% sequentially or 55% y/y to $307M, the company’s fastest sequential acceleration in accounts receivable in 10 quarters.

Lumentum’s exposure to the high-margin data center networking market has allowed the company’s gross margins to return to the pre-pandemic range of ~31% while the company returns to profitability on an operating basis.

Lumentum’s Valuation Multiples May Look Rich But Worth It

At the moment, Lumentum’s valuations may look rich at ~63x FY26 EPS for the year ending June 2026 or at 40x FY27 EPS. Compare that valuation of 63x to FY26 EPS growth of 184%, and I believe 63x looks attractive. With the possibility of more hyperscaler customers and/or chipmakers ramping up purchase orders of Lumentum’s networking components, Lumentum could well exceed market expectations of 61% revenue growth in FY26.

On top of that, Lumentum’s dominant market position in laser components and systems gives the company enough reason to raise prices of its components in the face of heightened demand for its lasers. Lumentum’s management has begun consistently hinting that they are “completely sold out” of their product, giving more credibility to analyst reports that Lumentum could raise prices for its EML lasers. This will likely boost ASPs and gross margins and give a meaningful boost to Lumentum’s EPS growth beyond the 184% expected EPS growth in FY26.

With that backdrop in mind, investors can view Lumentum’s earnings multiples of 63x as appealing.

Risks & Other Factors To Consider

Lumentum is a meaningfully indebted company with $1.21B in cash and $3.3B in debt. Assuming EBITDA margins expand to 26-27%, I estimate Lumentum’s gross leverage to be at 4.7x, which is elevated. Therefore, if Lumentum does trip on execution errors or AI networking demand dilution, Lumentum’s valuation multiples will quickly see significant pressure.

Lumentum is also set on reporting its Q2 FY26 ER next week on 3rd February. Here’s what I’m looking for:

  • Any further increase in Lumentum’s accounts receivable that signals sustained acceleration?
  • Can Lumentum guide Q3 EPS above midpoint expectations of $1.58 and Q3 revenues of $707M?
  • Any new customer wins or program wins or accelerated ramps in Nvidia’s switch deployments?

Takeaway

2026 will most likely be the year of rising adoption of optical components, lasers, and solutions for AI networking as trends like CPO and photonics command more usability.

In such scenarios, Lumentum stands to benefit from surging demand for its components and solutions supported by its market-dominant position for laser components.

I am initiating my views on Lumentum with a bullish rating.

data.title: Siemens Healthineers: Potentially Much Stronger Than Siemens In 2026E
data.description:
Whenever I update a company frequently, such as I am now doing with Siemens Healthineers (SEMHF), it's because I see something in that company that I want you to pay attention to. Usually, because I am a value-oriented investor, that something has to do with the valuation and the relative upside of a business even at a discount.

Sometimes, and this is not often, the company offers not only a good upside, it offers a very good safety in conjunction with that upside. That's true in this case as well.

Siemens Healthineers is a world-class meditech/healthcare company, backed up by Siemens (SIEGY) and its credit rating as well as significant shareholding. The company used to be one of Siemens' crown jewels", and was much of the reason why Siemens was considered an innovative and creative company. It has, for decades and decades, been a leader in imaging (medical), therapies and diagnostic tools. Its separation came as a surprise to me in 2016, but it's something I understood more of when looking at Siemens new strategic approach. In my view, Siemens is now overvalued (I very recently covered the parent in an article), while Siemens Healthineers is not - it is in fact undervalued, and one of my biggest picks for the coming year.

Investing in this market is far from easy. That makes it so much more important to know what companies are attractive, and why we buy them. If you were to buy the "wrong" companies here (as I see them), you're putting yourself in a position of a potential double-digit downturn for the long time. By investing in the right ones, even in the case of a market crash (which I consider to now be likely), you're safe because of the fundamentals and operational safety of your holdings, added to by solid dividends. That's my entire goal with investing - safe capital allocation for the short, medium and long term, and its what I help my clients with, and try to show you here in my articles.

Let me show you why Siemens Healthineers is such an important component of this strategy.

Siemens Healthineers upside - even in conservative forecasts, I consider this investment to be safe.

The first question that followers and investors seem to have for this company is why, if it's so great, is the company currently underperforming in such a volatile manner. That is a good question. In my risk/considerations I go through some of these, but let me explain it more succinctly.

The reason why the company is underperforming is primarily geopolitical. What this means is Healthineers initially saw promising growth in China, but this has not turned out the way the company expected it to. You could also mention things like inflation, FX and macro/tariffs, but those are more forces of nature - things Healthineers can't really affect.

If you follow my articles, you know that I view the Chinese market with an unfavorable perspective. I've long taken a bearish view on any company's estimates for growth and profit here. I, in fact, believe that many analysts and companies severely overestimate what this market is capable of today. The signals that I see, coming the form of demographic declines, significant debt, imbalances and a clear economic slow-down.

I, in fact, believe that you could call China's economic growth as having reached a "dead end", at least insofar as external (non-Chinese) companies expect continued, double-digit rates of growth.

So know that when I put my targets for SEMHF, I am severely discounting China's growth estimates. If you are (though I don't believe you should be), more bullish on China, you can go ahead and up those estimates by quite a bit. But in doing so, you should have explanations and guidance for Chinese challenges, such as proven lower consumption, a real estate/deflation crisis, and the severe fundamental and high-level challenges that the nation is facing.

This is the primary risk to SEMHF, and why the stock is so volatile. Not, I argue, the actual underlying performance - because ex-China, that performance is solid. For 2026, the company is expecting 5-6% revenue growth, as well as a flat to stable (or slightly negative) EPS. 2026 is therefore a transition year, and a chance to get into SEMHF on the "ground floor", as it remains priced competitively.

The mid-term outlook for the company is much better. If you look beyond 2026, we're expecting 6-10% top-line growth, related to precision therapy and imaging growth, as well as recovery in diagnostics, which would, at current margins, lead to double-digit adjusted EPS growth.

Even with a low yield at 2.3%, that sort of improvement would drive a higher valuation and share price, which would put you as the potential investor in a very good position. The company's current P/E is around 18x, average, but this ignores the 10-year average P/E completely, because this is more than 35% above this.

I therefore believe the current muted market outlook or interest for the company, related to both macro and economic trends, as well as the issues in China, can be an opportunity for savvy investors to buy the company cheaply. I started investing more seriously in the company back in April of 2025, again in November, during my last article, and now I'm looking to buy even more. The attentive ones will see that I "time" my investments to a very low P/E and price, something I do with continual pricing alerts on my broker. In this case, the alert was set at €44/share, a level the company dropped below on Friday, the 23rd of January.

I believe the operational and fundamental reasons for the company's outperformance in 2026 are related in part to it's market-dominating operational positions in key treatment areas. It's also been pushing into new areas, such as healthcare AI, and has been working on integrating its recent massive M&A, Varian, oncology-related.

Despite all of the risks in China that are mentioned and focused on, I believe that the company is likely to outperform in 2026. I'm hardly alone with this as well. Specifically, I believe that Imaging, one of the highest margin segments, and a recovery in diagnostics, will result in significant growth. I also believe the market is severely underestimating the Varian segment when you put it into the context of AI-based automation (or at the very least partial automation).

The fact that Healthineers continues to exhibit extremely strong partnerships further cements the business's position and upside, including recent partnerships with Nova Scotia Health, with Stryker and with others.

It puts the company in a very compelling situation for valuation.

Valuation for Siemens Healthineers - Upside in any historically-based forward multiple

Even basing the forecast on a sub-20x P/E, you get a 12-13% upside based on these estimates.

Why is this significant or important?

Because there's no historical support for a forward valuation this low - especially at the growth that the company is forecasting here. Even if we discount the company's growth based on lower China returns, the current valuation for this world-leading medtech provider doesn't really make sense. Even half the growth at 22-24x P/E would result in 15%+ annualized inclusive of the yield. At current estimates, the 10-year average gives us closer to 25% per year, implying a fair value of at least €73/share, close to 80% higher than the current level. Remember that part of this growth estimate is not at all China, but instead recovery in diagnosis, growth in imaging, and upside from the "proven" growth of Varian. This gives Siemens Healthineers a material and objective portfolio breadth compared to many of its competitor businesses. The same thing is true with Imaging.

Plenty of companies manufacture imaging equipment. Siemens margins are just significantly better, owing to both scale and market share - this is part of what's driving the upside here. And this is not even including impacts from the recent Atellica platform.

I continue to view the company's fair value at close to €62-€65/share, and this takes 100% of China's growth or recovery out of the equation. In the event of any significant China growth, that upside would go to €80/share. The PT in my last article for the company was €53/share, and I'm not changing that here. That means the company is currently and clearly trading well below its fair value.

In any scenario, I see significant upside to the company here. It's a safer choice than Carl Zeiss (CZMWY), Philips (PHG) as well as Elekta, out of Sweden.

Risks are as follows.

Risks and Considerations

Central risks to the company are linked to China exposure, and expectations for China recovery. I see such recovery as being unlikely, at the level it's being forecasted, and that is why I consider the company at a substantially lower price target than most seem to be doing here.

Operational risks for the company are few and far between, but I'll continue to keep an eye on the diagnostics turnaround and the APAC segment (China again - I'm not saying upside is completely unlikely, but I do say that it's tricky here). Aside from that, on the operational side, we have the diagnostics segment, which is seeing increased competition from relatively entrenched and capable players in the market, such as Abbott (ABT) and Roche. These also have scale advantages that are larger than Healthineers, which means that growth in diagnostics could be seeing more challenges than anticipated.

Also, as a final consideration, the company's Ultrasound business line has not done all that well for some time. I still believe, as I've said in previous articles, that the segment might be a good target for monetization/liquidation, but so far the company is sticking with it.

At this time, Siemens Healthineers continues to be a Buy. The target for the ADR, SEMHF, comes to $61.5 as of the latest FX.

Thesis

Healthineers is one of several class-leading companies in the medtech/equipment sector. While not the highest yield available (though above 2.3% now), it presents an appealing thesis, with the potential for a double-digit upside at a parent's (Siemens) credit safety of A+/A1. 2026 doesn't change the thesis for the company, and I continue to see an above-average market potential for the business.

I maintain my previously adjusted share price target for the company. Diagnostics remains the biggest challenge for the company, with upsides in Varian now included and adjusted/forecasted for.

My 2026 PT for Siemens Healthineers comes to €53/share, with a fair value upside of 15% annualized to a price of over €65/share. This means that the company is not yet "cheap" (which would be below €42/share), but it's still, in my view, one of the best alternatives in the global medtech sector. With upsides from Varian and Imaging, I believe the company is able of a 5-7% adjusted EPS growth even without China recovery - and that is why I continue to rate Siemens Healthineers at a "BUY" rating.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of €53/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I rate the company "BUY" as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XDTE: Here's How I'm Utilizing This High Yield Fund In2026
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (XDTE), I issued a hold rating due to the high risk tradeoffs of the fund. The fund continues to offer a high starting dividend yield of ~21%, while issuing payouts on a weekly basis. However, the underlying issue of the fund's likelihood to erode investor capital over a longer holding period isn't ideal. Now that we've crossed into the new year and the S&P 500 Index (SPX) continues to hit new all-time highs, I wanted to revisit XDTE to discuss how the fund may be efficiently used during a continued bull market. I also wanted to share how the fund may perform if we see a downward shift in momentum of the index.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that XDTE's share price has declined by nearly 21.3%. This is expected for such a high yielding fund, but I admittedly would like to see some improvement in the resilience of the share price in the future. Unfortunately, the portfolio structure just doesn't make this likely over a longer holding period. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to nearly 10.3% over the same time frame. While this may not be impressive at first glance, we should still consider that XDTE's total return has outpaced the S&P 500 since the time of my last coverage. This can be attributed to the high distributions paid out, including the special year-end supplemental that was recently paid.

Despite XDTE's strategy being vulnerable to a capped upside growth, I believe that the fund can still make sense within a diversified portfolio. However, there are many risks that investors should consider before initiating a position. Timing is very important with these 0DTE (zero-days-to-expiration) funds, so I will be detailing when I choose to accumulate shares, versus when I let the position sit uninterrupted. So let's start by reviewing the underlying strategy that XDTE implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's latest overview, XDTE now has total assets under management of $388M. The fund also has a gross expense ratio of 0.97%, which may seem high but is worth it considering the active management and high yield offered to investors. In order to generate the high level of income needed to support distributions, XDTE implements a synthetic option writing approach on the S&P 500. So instead of holding an ETF or the individual holdings within the index, XDTE is writing options against an asset that it doesn't actually own.

So in order to replicate the daily price movement of the index, XDTE purchases deep ITM (in-the-money) call options on the S&P 500 Index. As we can see in the fund's holdings below, XDTE simply holds these call options alongside the Roundhill Weekly T-Bill ETF (WEEK), which helps the fund maintain a healthy level of liquidity. These call options are typically dated very far out in terms of its expiration date.

  • SPX 03/19/2027 - $678.8C
  • SPX 12/18/2026 - $663.6C

Accompanying these long-dated synthetic calls, XDTE layers in a 0DTE strategy that allows a high level of option premiums to be captured. This means that these options reset daily since the positions expire the same afternoon at market close. The benefit here is that since the positions expire every day, this effectively resets the capped nature of the fund. Traditionally, these OTM (out-of-the-money) call options cap a fund's growth to whatever the selected strike price is. Since the strike price is continually being reset every day, this allows the fund to capture overnight upward gaps in the market.

This strategy separates XDTE from other option ETFs that have longer-dated options that may have already capped the upside growth to the selected strike price. XDTE's approach allows it to fully participate in the overnight movement of the index and when a new trading day is started, the upside cap is reset and adjusted to match current market conditions.

Implementation & Weakness

However, investors shouldn't let the large yield cloud the reality of this fund. I currently hold XDTE but I use it as an accompanying position to an already diversified portfolio of assets. Just because XDTE has a large dividend yield, this doesn't automatically translate to high total returns over time. Since the fund's inception, we can see that the share price has eroded by 23.68%. When including all distributions that were paid out, the fund has a total return of 33.01%. While this remains within reach of the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), we can see that XDTE underperforms despite its massive yield.

Looking at XDTE's price above, we can see that most of this erosion occurred following the rapid market decline that happened throughout March and April of 2025. This market drop was in response to President Trump's tariffs and this demonstrates one of the main risks of the fund's strategy. The 0DTE approach is great during bull markets, but it can be very fatal to the fund's success during a market decline because it restricts the fund's ability to meaningfully participate in a sharp recovery. So if the S&P 500 rallied higher by 2% per day for example, XDTE may have only been able to partially participate in this.

Over time, this creates a permanent disconnect in the fund that it cannot recover from. Even if markets rallied higher throughout 2026, I do not anticipate the share price to get back to its prior highs. Therefore, investors should anticipate that essentially 100% of their returns will come from XDTE's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions. From the market's bottom on April 8, 2025, SPY experienced a rally of more than 37%. If XDTE is unable to capture even a tenth of this amount, it helps shape the reality of how much of a bull market is needed for the fund to see rapid price growth.

With these risks in mind, here is the strategy I am implementing over the course of 2026. Firstly, I do not blindly reinvest distributions received from XDTE. Instead, I let the weekly distributions accumulate as cash and then I manually deploy them based on a few different market signals.

Here are the guidelines I follow:

  1. I only add to my existing position on red days. If the S&P 500 is declining and the S&P VIX Index (VIX) is spiking, I accumulate shares.
  2. I avoid adding shares during a multi-day bull market because I know there's a high probability that XDTE's share price will eventually decline. Therefore, I am not adding shares at this time.
  3. I will aggressively add if market fear rises, driven by factors such as geopolitical conflict, inflationary data, or other macroeconomic factors. When volatility spikes, the potential for high option premiums rise.
  4. Most importantly, XDTE is used as an accompanying position within a diversified portfolio of assets. This includes traditional growth ETFs, individual growth holdings, and other income funds. Since XDTE is unlikely to grow over time, I need to offset NAV erosion with growth somehow.

Unfortunately, NAV erosion is just a part of the fund's reality due to the generous distribution. The fund may frequently find itself in a scenario where the option premiums cannot support the annualized yield, so XDTE may occasionally pay out more than it actually earns. This problem only exacerbates the issue of a declining share price over a longer holding period.

Dividend Utility

As of the latest declared weekly distribution of $0.1612 per share, the current dividend yield sits around ~21%. Although the yield is high, investors must consider that a new payout is declared every single week. Therefore, the estimated annual yield can frequently change and will likely be different by the time you read this. Looking back over the fund's payout history, XDTE has a history of rewarding shareholders with a large year-end supplemental payout when market conditions allow. The last supplemental was massive and increases the appeal for income hungry investors.

Additionally, the weekly frequency can be useful for strategic portfolio management because it means that investors can compound their income more rapidly. For instance, the weekly payouts means that I frequently have more capital on hand to take advantage of market opportunities. For instance, I have been manually reinvesting some of my XDTE distributions into growth holdings, such as Meta Platforms (META).

The ironic nature of the fund is that a bull market throughout 2026 may translate to lower implied volatility. If implied volatility is lower, there's also a chance that the actual payouts can slightly decline. Conversely, XDTE's distribution amounts are also likely to decline if the market momentum reverses. If the S&P 500 declines, the fund may not produce consistent option premiums. Similarly, the fund's underlying net assets will decrease, which means there is now a smaller pool of capital that XDTE can deploy.

What I like most about XDTE's payouts is that the fund prioritizes return of capital distributions. For instance, the latest section 19(a) notice indicates that 100% of the payouts on a YTD basis were classified as return of capital. This is ideal because return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, these distributions reduce an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. An investor would only pay taxes on the capital gains, assuming there are any.

However, the use of return of capital has a direct impact on XDTE's NAV. So if the fund issues a $0.50 distribution that is entirely made up of return of capital, the share price will drop by the same amount. When you pair this concept with the fact that upside growth is capped by the options, it helps emphasize how unlikely it is for XDTE to provide a meaningful level of price growth over time.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on XDTE at this time. I consider XDTE to be a high risk income fund that requires more active management, rather than a simple buy-and-hold approach. I only accumulate shares of XDTE during sizeable market pullbacks, while also refusing to accumulate during market rallies. The underlying structure means that XDTE is very unlikely to deliver any meaningful price growth over time, so investors should anticipate that essentially all of their returns will come from the fund's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions. The actual distribution amounts may vary over time since they are influenced by market momentum and implied volatility levels. However, the use of return of capital is attractive because it limits the tax liabilities for investors.

data.title: Brown & Brown: Great Insurance Broker's Discount Is Better Now
data.description:
Brown & Brown, Inc. (BRO) just released Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings. While they reported year-over-year growth, the stock was down on the news the next day. I rated it a Buy before, at $95, and I don't see whatever disappointing news the market sees.

Summary of Previous Thesis

When I wrote about B&B last time, I rated it a Buy because I liked their growing, profitable business. A global insurance broker, they can grow without bearing the risk of being an underwriter.

With acquisitions, they can scoop up books of business without too much cost. Their scale makes them appealing to underwriters around the world, giving them tools and data to improve their own risk models. M&A is their primary form of capex, as opposed to standard additions to their own property and equipment.

With a Discounted Cash Flow model of their FCF per share (counting M&A as capex), I determined its intrinsic value was $114 per share. With a discount and a healthy balance sheet on a grower, I believed the stock was attractive and rated it a Buy.

Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results

If there is a primary reason for the stock's price decline, it's likely the decline in organic revenue for the fourth quarter.

In the earnings call, management primarily attributed these declines to:

  • $28M of non-recurring flood claims processing.
  • Larger decline in CAT rates (than expected).
  • Ongoing slowdown in lender-placed business.
  • Binding authority business is shifting back to the admitted market.

Overall, management considered these impacts contained and short-term, not concerning for the long-term business.

Nevertheless, full-year results showed both revenue growth and adjusted EPS growth.

Seen here, we see several expense items worked out, such as acquisition and integration costs. For my part, I think these should not be counted. After all, M&A is a major part of their strategy. This is why I think free cash flow (counting M&A as capex) is a better measure of value and financial performance.

When I wrote last time, I surmised that the acquisition of Accession could raise FCF (ahead of M&A) to $1.4B, and we got that for the full-year results. Obviously, the cash portion of the transaction (financed in part by equity and debt) created negative cash flow for the year, but this was an exceptionally large transaction.

This grew the end-of-year balance of long-term debt from $3.6B to $6.9B. While this is a sizable increase, let's look again at the debt maturities.

Referring to the latest Form 10Q again, pg. 42 (the 2025 Form 10K is not released yet), most of this balance is due more than five years out. Cash generation is sufficient to repay this or to position the company favorably for refinancing.

M&A continues into 2026 as well, with the following deals announced:

  • Shoemaker & Besser.
  • Sure Insurance Services.

These things continue to drive cash generation and growth of FCF per share.

Outlook and Valuation

Management gave a mixed outlook for rates and revenue coming into 2026. CEO J. Powell Brown noted:

From a pricing standpoint, we expect admitted rates to be fairly similar to what we experienced in the fourth quarter or might moderate slightly. We believe casualty rates will continue to increase, which are the largest segment of the market and that admitted property will continue to be competitively priced. For the E&S space, we anticipate pricing will be very similar to the fourth quarter, with casualty lines being the most challenging to place. Due to the lack of meaningful insured losses from hurricanes last year and the amount of available capital, we believe CAT property rates will decline modestly from the levels in the fourth quarter.

Long-term growth is therefore largely dependent on the execution of their M&A strategy and the compounding it creates through these "tuck-ins." Organic growth rates will be affected by the distinct ebbs and flows of their different business lines. Among existing customers, he also noted:

Our customers for the most part continue to hire at a modest pace and invested in their businesses as they see steady demand for their products and services. Not all industries are equal as some companies are hiring while others are holding steady, and we're not seeing any major workforce reductions impacting our diversified customer base.

There are, of course, some risk factors to challenge growth aspects. The earnings call began with an acknowledgment that some of their talent had been "poached," specifically 275 members who were responsible for $23M in revenue. The company explained it is pursuing litigation against these actions, but this is still an impact.

They also noted the impact of rate softening, particularly in the absence of major storms in a year like 2025. Brown stated (emphasis added):

So I believe that we're going to continue to have some pretty significant competition on those rates in the near to intermediate term. And I would typically say that really exists down between now and May or June, and then you get into hurricane season. So I would tend to say that I think it's still going to be quite competitive between now and then.

Nevertheless, I think this speaks to the normal environment in which B&B has always competed and has always managed to grow. While it may mean lower earnings in the near term, strong and well-capitalized brokers tend to win out in the long run when pennies are pinched.

Returning to my valuation, I'll update it with new FCF per share based on:

  • $1,450M in 2025's operating cash flow.
  • $750M as a baseline for M&A (up from my $700M last time).
  • Current shares outstanding at 336M.
  • Giving FCF per share of $2.08.

My long-term growth rates stem from the M&A pipeline, which is consistently robust for them and naturally supports cash generation with improving economies of scale. Organic growth should generally be positive over time as well. According to Precedence Research, the global insurance brokerage market size is currently projected to grow from $343B in 2025 to $818B by 2035.

Even if it only grows half as much, B&B is positioned to ride this and to capture a larger piece of it through their M&A strategy.

Overall, I think the price decline to about $75 is very attractive, and short-term revenue bumps in consistently profitable growers are where investors make their money.

Conclusion

B&B might have some short-term hiccups, but I think these are normal, something that the company has experienced before throughout its long history of acquiring and compounding. As they grow, they become better positioned to capture new business. With the long-term picture intact, I confidently maintain my BRO Buy rating.

data.title: Discovery Silver: Riding On Bullish Precious Metal Pricing And Robust Operational Performance
data.description:
The precious metal markets have had a considerable run over the past year, with spot gold up 85.75% (YoY) while the price of spot silver has surged 256.30% (YoY). Early in Q2 2025, global royalty and stream company, Franco-Nevada Corporation (FNV), made an equity investment in Canadian precious metals explorer, Discovery Silver Corp (DSVSF) that included, among other aspects, a 4.25% net smelter return (NSR) royalty on its Porcupine complex. DSVSF is UP 987.67% (YoY), meaning that FNV has gained a return of more than 260% since April 2025. In its Q3 2025 report, FNV stated that it sold a part of its DSVSF equity investment, attaining a profit of $67.4 million from a total income of $84.4 million. FNV sold $17 million worth of its equity at DSVSF, and the profit represented a growth of almost 400%. I will discuss why I am rating DSVSF as a hold, as it is trading within its fair valuation while demonstrating robust operating performance into 2026 amid positive resource development and high potential growth.

Discovery Silver has 3 operational mines (at Porcupine Complex) with 2 mines at Hoyle Pond (multiple high-grade intersections) and 1 mine at Borden. As at Q4 2025 (in the report released in mid-January 2026), Discovery stated that the Pamour Mine (yet to produce commercial gold) had “excellent drilling results in all its three phases of pit designs.” The reports show that Discovery aims to advance operations within the new Pamour mine, with production expected to come online in 2026/2027.

Gold production at DSVSF grew 5.64% (QoQ) in Q4 2024 to 66,718 ounces as compared to 63,154 ounces in Q3 2025. Of the 66,718 ounces of gold produced in Q4 2025, DSVSF sold 64,479 ounces at an average price of $4,157/ ounce, representing a price increase of 19.15% (QoQ). Since gold is the main revenue earner of the company, it means DSVSF expects at least $268.04 million in Q4 2024 (+13.12%, QoQ). After acquiring Porcupine from Newmont in April 2025, Discovery Silver has made about $647 million (in total revenues from Q2 2025- $142 million, in Q3 2025-$237 million and in Q4 2025- $268.04 million) giving a profit of 52.24% (from the initial purchase price of $425 million- in cash, shares and deferred payments).

In late 2025, Discovery announced that it had completed 27,640 meters of its 140,000-meter drilling exploration project, meaning that it was getting into 2026 with a balance of 112,350 meters (from the Porcupine Complex). The full year 2025 is expected to see a total gold production of 234,702 ounces, with about 180,424 ounces being from April 2025 to December 2025. Then again, gold prices have been on a bullish run recently, almost touching the $5,000/ounce mark. Price forecasts for gold have been set at $5,400, with analysts seeing 2026 as another strong year for gold. For Discovery, this trajectory is a pivot for the company, having come from just being a silver developer to a solid gold producer in less than a year (after the acquisition of the Porcupine Complex).

Robust liquidity continues to be key in the company’s growth plans, with cash at $410 million (as of Q4 2025), up 20% (QoQ). By Q3 2025, it had a working capital of $224.2 million, a revolving credit line of $250 million that was to be topped up to $350 million in Q4 2025 (an additional $100 million). Save for the revolving credit line, which the company had not borrowed into 2026, Discovery has no debt.

At this point, I would like to calculate DSVSF’s net burn into 2026, keeping in mind that it is progressing its permitting process in Cordero with additional development plans in Pamour. I will calculate the company’s cash usage as of Q4 2025. In the 12 months trailing to September 30, 2025, cash generated from DSVSF’s operations stood at $211.8 million against invested capital (CAPEX) of $361.7 million. The difference gives us the total expenses or net burn equaling to $$149.9 million accrued in 9 months. DSVSF’s burns about $17 million per month. As stated earlier, the company’s working capital as of Q3 2025 stood at $224.2 million. To get the cash runway per month, I will divide the working capital ($224.2 million) by the monthly net burn ($17 million), which equals 13.2 months. The company has enough cash to run its operations until late Q4 2026.

Valuation

I will use the enterprise value against sales (EV/Sales) multiple in evaluating DSVSF, as it will factor in the company’s value (including debt and cash) against revenue. I had indicated earlier that DSVSF expects a revenue of about $647 million with a cash position of $410 million and zero debt.

The forward EV/Sales ratio stands at 10.43X. I will calculate the future enterprise value by multiplying this ratio (10.43X) by the forward revenue ($647 million), giving us $6.75 billion. I will then calculate the market capitalization by adding DSVSF’s cash ($410 million) to the EV ($6.75 billion), which equals to $7.16 billion (keeping in mind that the company has no debt). DSVSF’s future price will be calculated by dividing the market capitalization of $7.16 billion by the total shares outstanding as of September 30, 2025, which is 806.9 million shares, equaling to $8.87. DSVSF’s share price as of this writing stood at $8.49, indicating a difference of +4.52% (YoY).

Key milestones that could magnify this valuation in my view are 1) permit approval and positive progress in silver resource development at its Cordero mine in Mexico, and 2) successful gold mining operations at the Pamour mine to supplement production at Hoyle Pond and Borden in 2026.

I will compare the performance of Discovery with some of its peers in the gold and silver mining industry.

Fwd. EV/Sales Fwd. Sales Fwd. EV Future Market Cap Future Share Price Current share price Valuation (difference) Current Momentum
DSVSF 10.43X $647 million $6.75 billion $7.16 billion $8.89 $8.49 +4.52% (YoY) trading at a fair valuation +1,220.43% (YoY)
Endeavour Silver Corp. (EDR: CA) 8.91X $469.1 million $4.2 billion $4.06 billion $13.79 $19.33 -40.17% (YoY) overvalued +274.61% (YoY)
First Majestic Silver Corp. (AG) 10.99X $965.6 million $10.612 billion $10.95 billion $22.29 $25.44 -14.13% (YoY) overvalued +353.48% (YoY)

Of the 3 stocks analyzed in the table above, only Discovery Silver is trading at a fair valuation with a potential upside of about 4.5% (YoY). It also has the strongest share momentum over the past year, with an upside of over 1200% (YoY) into 2026.

Risks

DSVSF’s valuation is built on the premise that it will sell the intended gold sums at the predicted prices (see here). Failure to attain this goal will hurt the company’s revenue and adversely affect the share price. There are also key milestones to be achieved, such as positive operation performance at the new Pamour mine and the silver resource development plans at Cordero. Failure to attain these milestones will also adversely affect the share price in 2026.

Bottom Line

I have rated Discovery Silver Corp as a hold as it is trading at a fair valuation while showing robust operating performance into 2026 amid positive resource development and high potential growth. I expect the company to attain a revenue of $647 million for the FY 2025, based on the recent calculation of its Q4 2025 earnings results. It also has a cash runway of 13.2 months, meaning it can sustain its operations until late Q4 2026. Additionally, the strong share momentum over the past year is supported by market optimism regarding the positive performance of its Porcupine Complex, as well as optimism surrounding the Pamour mine and Cordero.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cooper-Standard Holdings: When Price Isn't The Only Thing That Matters
data.description:
I don't know about you, but I have an appreciation for the automotive sector. Much of the modern economy was built around the automobile. And anything that makes this economy move is of interest to me. It's also a sector that, even though it's prone to disruption, will only continue to grow from here on out. Or at least it will, absent some major change in technology or demographics.

One company in this space that is worth knowing about is Cooper-Standard Holdings (CPS). But there is a difference between worth knowing about it and it being an attractive prospect. In some ways, the company is appealing. For instance, shares are trading on the cheap. On the other hand, financial performance has been volatile, and sales as of late have been declining. When you add on top of this the fact that economic conditions are worsening, and that automotive sales are likely to be lower this year than they were last year, I believe that the most generous rating that I can assign the company is a "Hold."

Taking Cooper-Standard Holdings for a drive

The management team at Cooper-Standard Holdings describes the company as a producer of sealing and fluid handling systems. Examples here include fuel and brake delivery systems and fluid transfer systems. These products are mostly used in passenger vehicles and light trucks. Overwhelmingly, the company caters to the OEM space, with 86% of its revenue in 2024 coming from players there such as Ford Motor Company (F), General Motors (GM), and others. And while the company does have a significant presence in the United States, it does have some foreign operations as well. Specifically, I'm talking about China, where some of its major customers include BYD, Geely, and Chery.

The company does generate some revenue outside of the OEM space. In fact, 14% of sales came from automotive suppliers, non-automotive customers, and replacement part distributors. Given the fact that individuals are keeping their vehicles longer than ever before, which should increase demand for aftermarket parts, I would actually prefer that the business operate as an enterprise centered around the aftermarket industry. But on the whole, this does not invalidate it as an investment opportunity.

In recent years, Cooper-Standard Holdings has exhibited mixed financial performance. Revenue increased from $2.53 billion in 2022 to $2.82 billion in 2023. But then, in 2024, revenue dropped to $2.73 billion. The company's revenue is highly correlated to the light vehicle production market. And I don't just mean that for the United States. I mean it globally as well. In 2023, North American light vehicle production amounted to 15.7 million units. In 2024, that number dropped to 15.5 million. Most other markets saw increases. But not all of them. Europe, for instance, saw a decline from 18 million vehicles to 17.1 million. At the time that management announced results for the 2024 fiscal year as a whole, they expected declining sales in both North America and Europe for 2025. I don't have any reliable data regarding Europe. But I do know that, in the United States, we actually saw an increase to 16.2 million new vehicle sales. This far exceeded the 15.1 million that management was anticipating for North America.

Unfortunately, as the first chart in this article illustrates, bottom line performance for the business has been all over the map. Seeing as how its customers are largely big players in the automotive space, exercising significant influence over their own supply chains, and considering that companies such as Cooper-Standard Holdings typically have to abide by longer-term contracts, and that we went through a window of inflationary pain, it's not unreasonable to see mixed financial performance. More important than what happened over the last couple of years, however, is what happened last year.

During the first nine months of 2025, revenue for the company was virtually flat compared to the same time a year earlier. It amounted to about $2.07 billion. This seems to have been driven by a couple of things, including foreign currency fluctuations and increases in volume and product mix. We do know that, in China, light vehicle production jumped 11.7% year over year, from 20.6 million vehicles to 23 million. But outside of China and South America, there was some weakness. Even though new vehicle sales in the United States ended up being higher in 2025, actual production numbers dropped during the first nine months to 11.7 million from the 11.8 million that we saw the previous year. In Europe, production declined from 12.8 million to 12.6 million.

Bottom line results are worthy of our attention. The company went from a net loss of $119 million to a net loss of only $7.5 million. A reduction in restructuring charges of $11.9 million played a role here. But the bigger contributor was the fact that, during the first nine months of 2024, the company was hit by a $44.6 million pension settlement charge that did not repeat in 2025. Other profitability metrics ended up being positive, surprisingly. Operating cash flow jumped from $1.6 million to $8.2 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $28.6 million to $83.2 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $136.7 million to $180.5 million.

For the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management said that investors should expect revenue of between $2.68 billion and $2.72 billion. Even at the high end, that would unfortunately represent a decline from the $2.73 billion reported for 2024. This would be the second consecutive year of revenue declines. Meanwhile, we should see some improvement in profitability, with EBITDA rising from $180.7 million in 2024 to between $200 million and $210 million in 2025. This would likely translate to an adjusted operating cash flow of about $49.3 million.

I would like to point out that these recent improvements that the company has captured have come from painstaking cost-cutting initiatives. Management has spent years rationalizing their manufacturing footprint by closing over a dozen manufacturing facilities and selling off some of its non-performing operations. It has centralized equipment specification and sourcing decisions, and has even reduced headcount. The company has also been open with investors about renegotiating supplier contracts. This is especially important in the era of tariffs. From 2019 through the third quarter of 2025, the company claims to have captured cumulative cost savings of about $715 million on an annual basis. A whopping $85 million of that came from the end of 2024 through the third quarter of 2025. So it is likely that some improvements will continue moving forward.

This is great in and of itself. However, that's not the only thing that should go into whether this makes for a good prospect. One item is valuation. Using the aforementioned estimates for 2025, as well as the results that the company achieved for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. In that same chart, I compared the company to five other similar firms. In addition to looking decently priced on an absolute basis, the firm is deeper than all but two of these competitors when it comes to the EV-to-EBITDA approach. But it’s cheaper than only one of the five when it comes to the price-to-operating-cash-flow approach.

The long-term picture for the company will likely be positive. According to management, these are rather sizable markets that it plays in. For instance, the global sealing systems market, which the company has a 16% stake in, is worth about $8.5 billion. Even larger is the fluid handling systems market. Management claims that the company is the second largest player on the planet here, with an 8.3% market share. However, this is a massive market, worth $14.4 billion in total. Management is trying to capture more of these markets by making investments in other opportunities. For instance, by focusing on newer innovative products, the company achieved $228.5 million of net new business awards during the first nine months of 2025. 87% of these related to innovative products. 83% involved battery electric or hybrid vehicle programs. And an impressive 62% focused on high growth Chinese OEMs.

Normally, these things would cause me to be at least a bit bullish. Having said that, I am concerned about where the economy is more broadly. As I have detailed in other articles, the United States seems to be heading toward a recession. China, as I detailed in another article, is also seeing its growth slow. Considering that, in the most recent quarter, 58.9% of the company's revenue came from North America while another 14.2% came from the Asia Pacific region, what goes on in both the United States and China is relevant to the business. We do know that, when it comes to the United States specifically, Cox Automotive forecasted that new vehicle sales this year will be about 15.8 million units. If this comes to fruition, it would be down from 2.4% compared to what the industry saw last year.
